mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E‑Class E350d / E400d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM656 engine typically costs £5,500–£8,500 supply only, with fitting adding £1,400–£2,000. A tandem oil/vacuum pump repair alone can cost over £12,000 at a main dealer. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your E‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E350d / E400d (OM656) engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM656 is a sophisticated and powerful straight‑six diesel, but it has several known issues. The tandem oil/vacuum pump failure is the most critical — it can cause loss of braking and requires an engine‑out repair. Cam rocker wear causes ticking/popping noises. AdBlue system failures are also common. With regular maintenance and early intervention, it can be a reliable engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the E350d / E400d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Tandem oil/vacuum pump failure (limp mode, loss of braking — safety critical), cam rocker wear (ticking/popping from intake), AdBlue system failures (sensors, heaters, SCR), turbo actuator vacuum hose collapse, increased oil consumption, and EGR valve clogging.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an E350d / E400d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and the nature of the failure. A W213 E350d / E400d is a £10,000–£25,000 vehicle — a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 24–70% of the car's value. For a tandem pump failure (up to £12,000 at a dealer), replacement may approach or exceed our 70% Mercedes scrap threshold. Evaluate carefully based on your car's condition and the specific repair needed.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E350d / E400d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The E350d and E400d engine code is OM656 — a 2.9‑litre straight‑six turbo‑diesel. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on an E350d / E400d?

acceptedAnswer.text: On the vacuum/oil pump: check for TSB completion — if the pump fails, it is an engine‑out job. On cam rocker wear: regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved MB 229.51/229.52 oil and shortened intervals (5,000–8,000 miles) are essential. On the AdBlue system: use high‑quality AdBlue from reputable sources and have the system serviced regularly. At the first sign of limp mode, ticking from the intake or AdBlue warnings, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes E‑Class E350d / E400d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current OM656 generation. A tandem oil/vacuum pump failure repair alone can cost upwards of £12,000 at a main dealer. A reconditioned engine is a more viable alternative in many cases.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E350d / E400d engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The OM656’s timing chain is at the back of the engine, making many repairs engine‑out jobs.

cta.label: → Compare E350d / E400d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Tandem Oil/Vacuum Pump Failure (OM656 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Limp mode, loss of braking assistance (servo failure), engine stalling, rough running, P0299E6 error code (boost pressure too low).
problems.repairCost: £1,500–£5,000+ (pump replacement, vacuum system clean, engine out)
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (full engine replacement if damage is extensive)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you experience loss of braking or limp mode, stop driving and book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM656’s tandem oil and vacuum pump is the single most critical issue. The seal between the oil and vacuum sides degrades over time, allowing oil to enter the vacuum system. This leads to limp mode and, in severe cases, complete loss of servo‑assisted braking. The repair requires the engine to be removed and can cost over £12,000 at a main dealer. If you experience any of these symptoms, do not drive the car — have it recovered to a specialist immediately. There is a TSB (Technical Service Bulletin) for this issue.

problems.title: Cam Rocker Wear / Camshaft Scoring (OM656 — All Years)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or popping noise from the intake, rough idle, loss of power, metallic ticking.
problems.replacementCost: £5,500–£9,500 (full engine replacement if damage is extensive)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear ticking or popping from the intake

problems.recommendation: Cam rocker wear is a known issue on the OM656. If left unaddressed, it can lead to scoring of the cam lobes, requiring expensive camshaft replacement. The popping noise from the intake is the early warning sign. Regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil (MB 229.51 or 229.52) are essential. If you hear a ticking or popping noise, have the valvetrain inspected immediately.

problems.title: AdBlue System Failures (All OM656)
problems.symptoms: Check engine light, AdBlue warning, reduced power, countdown to start restriction (usually 500 miles), engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £500–£2,500 (sensor replacement, AdBlue heater, SCR system repair)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if AdBlue countdown appears

problems.recommendation: The AdBlue (SCR) system on the OM656 is a common failure point. Faults include AdBlue level sensors (P203B31), concentration sensors (P206B31), and heaters. If the AdBlue system fails and the countdown reaches zero, the car will not start. Mercedes has issued technical bulletins for these issues. Use high‑quality AdBlue from reputable sources and have the system serviced regularly. At the first sign of an AdBlue warning, book an inspection immediately.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E350d / E400d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Engine‑out job — catch it early before complete failure
Catch it early — before cam lobes are scored
Sensor or heater replacement — use quality AdBlue

rows.problem: Severe engine damage (pump failure / chain jump)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E350d / E400d? Check These First
buyIf: * No history of vacuum/oil pump failure — or the pump has already been replaced under TSB
* No ticking/popping from the intake — cam rockers in good condition
* Full Mercedes service history with correct MB‑spec oil
* AdBlue system serviced — quality AdBlue used
avoidIf: * Vacuum/oil pump failure history with no documented repair — walk away
* Ticking or popping from the intake — cam rocker wear may be present
* AdBlue warning light with no diagnosis — potential SCR system failure
* Poor service history — OM656 engines are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The OM656 is one of Mercedes’ most advanced straight‑six diesels — smooth, powerful and efficient. But it has several well‑documented weaknesses: the tandem oil/vacuum pump (which can cause loss of braking), cam rocker wear and AdBlue system failures. The pump failure is safety‑critical and can occur at relatively low mileage. Buy on full service history, check for TSB completion and never ignore a ticking noise or AdBlue warning. A well‑maintained E350d / E400d is a rewarding, long‑lived executive express; a neglected one is a £10,000+ bill waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare E350d / E400d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes E‑Class E350d / E400d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Vacuum Pump — Check for any history of vacuum/oil pump failure — safety critical
* Cold Start — Listen for ticking or popping from the intake — cam rocker wear indicator
* AdBlue System — Check for warning lights — AdBlue system failure indicator
* Service History — including engine era (OM656) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo actuator hose issues possible
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler issues possible
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures (AdBlue/SCR)
